title: Coleus amiculatus
taxonomy: Coleus amiculatus is classified within the family Lamiaceae. This plant is commonly known as Coleus or Coleus amboinicus. Coleus amiculatus is an annual or perennial herb native to South America and the West Indies. It is a member of the mint family and is closely related to other species within the genus Plectranthus. The exact classification can be further broken down into the subfamily Nepetoideae and tribe Ocimeae.
uses: Coleus amiculatus is known for its potential medicinal properties, although more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. The plant has been traditionally used in some cultures to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its leaves contain compounds that have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may contribute to its medicinal benefits. In traditional medicine, Coleus amiculatus is sometimes used to treat digestive issues and as an antiseptic. Further studies are necessary to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential applications.
benefits: Coleus amiculatus, also known as Coleus forskohlii, has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for its potential health benefits. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate conditions such as arthritis and asthma. The plant is also thought to possess antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. Furthermore, it is believed to have anti-cancer properties, although more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness.

constituents: Coleus amiculatus contains various active constituents, including flavonoids, glycosides, and phenolic acids. intro: Coleus amboinicus, commonly known as Mexican oregano or Coleus amiculatus, is a perennial herb native to South America and Southeast Asia, characterized by its pungent, slightly bitter flavor and aroma. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its numerous health benefits, including its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antifungal properties, which can aid in treating various ailments such as digestive issues, fever, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Coleus amboinicus include essential oils, terpenes, and phenolic compounds, including carvacrol, limonene, and rosmarinic acid, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Coleus amboinicus is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, often combined with other herbs like ginger and peppermint to enhance its therapeutic effects.
